title: My experience with vibe coding>
summary: Vibe coding isn't a silver bullet
full_text:
Every once in a while I kick the tires on vibe coding, for various reasons:

sometimes I fall for the hype and guiltily reach for vibe coding when I'm stuck

other times someone tells me that the latest and greatest thing has turned a corner

I also want vibe coding or something like it to work (I'm generally pro-AI)

However, each time I try out vibe coding I'm unimpressed with the results. Don't get me wrong: I think vibe coding in its present state is pretty amazing and a cool tech demo but misses the mark as a software engineering tool.

I'll illustrate what I mean with a sample Claude Code session of mine. I picked Claude Code for my recent vibe coding trial because some of my coworkers and peers on social media were raving about it.

The challenge

Over the weekend I was hacking on my Grace project (a domain-specific language for prompt engineering1) and one of the issues I was dealing with is that the Nix build for GHCJS was failing with this error after upgrading to Nixpkgs 24.11:
Setup: Missing dependency on a foreign library:
* Missing (or bad) header file: ghc/utils/unlit/fs.h

Moreover, the failure was not reproducible2. The build succeeded on one machine but failed on another. I could work around that by copying the cached build product from one machine to another, but I wanted to better understand what was going on to gauge the full extent of the reproducibility problem.

I began debugging and after about an hour I had a "solution" (more like a workaround). Then I stopped and realized that this would actually be a good real-world example for me to trial vibe coding on because this is the sort of problem I deal with on the regular (both professionally and in my open source projects). I also had my own work that I could benchmark vibe coding against.

The result

So I subscribed to Claude Code Pro (for $17 / month), installed Claude Code, and fired it up in my terminal and began with this prompt:

I want to fix a build failure in my Nix configuration. To provide some context, this build does work on another machine, but for whatever reason doesn't work on this machine. Specifically the way to reproduce the build failure is to load the Nix shell by setting .envrc to "use flake '.#ghcjs'" and direnv reload or just nix build '.#website'.

… and then I let Claude run from there. The full session transcript is provided in the Appendix below, but my basic interaction policy was:

stay engaged/present (reading Claude's train of thought and commands)

intervene to clarify requirements

try not to bias it with knowledge I gathered from my own research

try to auto-approve as much as possible within reason

manually confirm all edits to the code

The only time I had to intervene was when Claude began to try to work around the issue by building for for x86_64-darwin instead of aarch64-darwin. My feedback was:

You don't need to run Rosetta. This build should definitely work on aarch64-darwin. I know that it works on another machine and pushing on an x86_64-darwin build is a dead end.

For other stuff I generally let Claude's ideas "ride" to see if Claude would learn and adjust on his own and to gauge to what extent I could let Claude work unsupervised.

Unfortunately, Claude's first pass at the solution was not good, and I'll paste it here:

This is pretty poor software engineering (I'll explain why in the next section), but I wanted to give Claude a chance to redeem himself so I started to get more hands on once I knew that Claude wasn't really cut out for "fire and forget" operation. However, I still avoided using my prior research and only gave feedback based on what Claude had gathered, learned, and presented to me.

First, I asked Claude to improve upon his solution:

So there are a few issues with this solution: (A) you shouldn't have hardcoded /nix/store paths (like /nix/store/rsd…-emdsk) and (B) you shouldn't copy/paste/edit the buildPhase (because that will lead to configuration drift if I, say, upgrade Nixpkgs)

… and then Claude went down a bad route (trying to use string replacement instead, which is also not good practice) so I proactively intervened again so that Claude wouldn't struggle going down that route:

❯ String replacement is also not an idiomatic Nix solution to the problem. Typically the right way to do these sorts of things is a well-placed .override(…) or .overrideAttrs(…) or similar

… and then Claude chugged on that for a while and eventually ran out of tokens for the day (because I was using the Pro plan and not the Max plan). This was after a ≈1 hour long session with Claude; I could upgrade to Claude Code Max which would give me ≈20× the pro plan so I could … spend a lot more money ($200 / month!) and time to teach Claude how to do a better job.

Needless to say I wasn't impressed. If I wanted to spend time and money on mentoring a junior engineer I'd rather spend that on teaching a human how to be a better software engineer3.

Shoddy engineering

I want to break down why Claude's solution was poor engineering because to an untrained eye it might seem like an acceptable solution to the problem. However, LLMs are notorious for generating solutions/ideas that seem good at first glance but then fall apart under scrutiny.

The first issue is: Claude's solution is not reproducible at all on other machines. In fact, it's not even necessarily reproducible on my own machine: Claude's solution will eventually stop working on my machine the first time I run a Nix garbage collection.

This is because Claude hard-codes the /nix/store/rsd7naynyq6r8cry568r1widlzgxvqzg-emsdk Nix store path, which is a big no-no in Nix4 for the reason I just mentioned (profound lack of reproducibility, even local reproducibility).

The other issue with the solution is that Claude couldn't figure out how to idiomatically patch the ghcjs build so Claude resorted to a big hack to work around its lack of knowledge:
+ buildPhase = ''
+ export HOME=$TMP
+ mkdir $HOME/.cabal
+ touch $HOME/.cabal/config
+ cd lib/boot
+
+ mkdir -p $out/bin
+ mkdir -p $out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7
+ lndir ${patchedBootGhcjs}/bin $out/bin
+ chmod -R +w $out/bin
+ rm $out/bin/ghcjs-boot
+ cp ${patchedBootGhcjs}/bin/ghcjs-boot $out/bin
+ rm $out/bin/haddock
+ cp ${patchedBootGhcjs}/bin/haddock $out/bin
+ cp ${patchedBootGhcjs}/bin/private-ghcjs-hsc2hs $out/bin/ghcjs-hsc2hs
+
+ wrapProgram $out/bin/ghcjs-boot --set ghcjs_libexecdir $out/bin
+
+ wrapProgram $out/bin/ghcjs --add-flags "-B$out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7"
+ wrapProgram $out/bin/haddock --add-flags "-B$out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7"
+ wrapProgram $out/bin/ghcjs-pkg --add-flags "--global-package-db=$out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7/package.conf.d"
+ wrapProgram $out/bin/ghcjs-hsc2hs --add-flags "-I$out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7/include --template=$out/lib/ghcjs-8.10.7/include/template-hsc.h"
+
+ env PATH=$out/bin:$PATH $out/bin/ghcjs-boot --with-emsdk=${emsdk} --no-haddock
+ '';

I happen to know (because of my own research on the problem) that Claude didn't thoughtfully craft that code; Claude copied/pasted/edited that code from here.

That's not good software engineering practice because if the upstream buildPhase changes the copy that Claude vendored in my flake.nix file won't pick up any of those changes. Moreover, it means that the derivation is highly likely to break when I upgrade (in the same way that pinning or vendoring a dependency tends to break upgrades). Again, this is the sort of issue that seems okay in the short-term but then causes issues in the long-term.

A much more direct way to do what Claude was trying to do would have been something like this:

index 465442e..f10cb22 100644
--- a/flake.nix
+++ b/flake.nix
@@ -16,6 +16,19 @@

+ compiler = super.haskell.compiler // {
+ ghcjs810 = super.haskell.compiler.ghcjs810.override (old: {
+ ghcjsDepOverrides = self: super: {
+ ghcjs = super.ghcjs.overrideAttrs (old: {
+ postPatch = (old.postPatch or "") +
+ ''
+ ./utils/makePackages.sh copy
+ '';
+ });
+ };
+ });
+ };
+

 "${compiler}" = super.haskell.packages."${compiler}".override (old: {
 overrides =

However, I know from my own debugging that neither Claude's solution nor that simpler equivalent actually fix the problem (it just delays the build failure to a new build failure that surfaces later). I'll cover the real problem and the fix in the next section.

I don't expect Claude to be perfect, but what I would have liked to see is for Claude to recognize that this code was brittle/unmaintainable and stopped and asked for my input/guidance before powering through with that approach. For example, Claude could have said "The solution I have in mind might not be maintainable for X, Y, and Z reasons. Are you okay with that? I might exhaust my token budget if I push on better solutions".

The bigger issue, though, is that someone unfamiliar with Nix could be misled into thinking that this solution was acceptable or even impressive! It "works" … until it doesn't. This is why I'm pretty skeptical of claims that vibe coding "levels up" people who are working in unfamiliar territory because they might not necessarily be qualified to understand the implications/tradeoffs/downsides of LLM-generated code (and vibe coding solutions don't seem to have gotten to the point where they're good at recognizing and communicating those tradeoffs). Every time I vibe code in an area within my own expertise I'm unimpressed with the results, which makes me highly skeptical that vibe coding does the right thing when I'm operating outside my comfort zone5.

Human solution

The actual cause of the build failure is a known issue in Nixpkgs which has a draft pull request open to address. Specifically:

The workaround I used was to run and cache the build using my existing Garnix CI (which runs on an older version of macOS which doesn't have this issue). This is still not ideal from a reproducibility standpoint, but this is about as reproducible as Darwin builds get in the Nixpkgs ecosystem. The best possible fix would be to take the above draft pull request across the line. An intermediate solution would be to change the gcc -E step to use a different C preprocessor (e.g. clang) just for that one failing command.

Conclusion

This example is pretty representative of my experiences using vibe coding. Every time I try out vibe coding for real (and not for demos) I start out feeling hopeful and excited and leave feeling let down and disappointed. I don't want to produce throwaway code: I want to build things that will last and won't blow up in my face later and I don't want to handhold/babysit/cajole the tool to get the results I want. I want vibe coding to improve the quality of my work, not deteriorate it.

I understand that's not how vibe coding is marketed by proponents. To quote Andrej Karpathy, the uh "vibe" of vibe coding is to:

… fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, and forget that the code even exists

… and that might work fine for tiny projects, but that doesn't really scale to projects of even a modest size or scope.

I don't think vibe coding is worthless and I do think the vibe coding experience can improve and has potential but vibe coding in its current incarnation is not the superhuman fantasy-land technology that some proponents make it out to be.

You also don't have to take my word for it: you can run this same experiment yourself. If you have Nix installed on macOS you can check out this branch of my Grace repository and ask your favorite vibe coding tool to fix the build failure and see what quality of solution you get.
